patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 24-year-old male IT support specialist. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by volunteering: “My feet and ankles feel weak, and I keep tripping when I walk,” plus shoe scuffing/toe catching, slow worsening over 2 years, and your lower legs look skinnier around shins/calves. If asked, describe difficulty heel-walking, lifting toes, clearing stairs unless concentrating, faster fatigue with long walks, mild hand weakness with jars/fine work, no real numbness/tingling, and recurrent ankle sprains from the foot turning in. Do not volunteer (unless specifically asked about gait/balance/impact): high-stepping to avoid tripping, worse balance on uneven ground, feet feel “floppy” after long days, avoiding certain shoes for ankle instability. Be somewhat resistant about falls/avoidance: admit only if asked directly, saying it’s embarrassing and you’ve avoided going out. Express misconceptions: worry it’s a pinched back nerve or being out of shape. Raise financial/privacy/job concerns about PT coverage, genetic testing cost/employer access, and workplace accommodations affecting promotion. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and become more open.

confounders: Regular weight training with recent increase in intensity (can elevate CK and mimic exertional myopathy)
Intermittent use of NSAIDs for musculoskeletal pain (may mask inflammatory symptoms and complicate interpretation of myalgia)
Family history of 'high arches' and ankle weakness not previously evaluated (may be misattributed to peripheral neuropathy such as Charcot-Marie-Tooth)
Vitamin D deficiency contributing to proximal muscle aches and fatigue, potentially obscuring primary distal weakness pattern

correct_diagnosis.primary: KLHL9-related early-onset distal myopathy
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary distal myopathy (genetic distal myopathy, unspecified gene)
Distal myopathy (unspecified type)
required_evidence: Progressive distal-predominant muscle weakness with early onset (childhood/adolescence/early adulthood) and slow progression
Family history consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance OR known pathogenic KLHL9 variant on genetic testing
Myopathic pattern on EMG and/or muscle biopsy compatible with myopathy (if performed)
No sensory loss and no upper motor neuron signs to suggest neuropathy or motor neuron disease
Labs showing normal to mildly elevated CK/aldolase (not markedly high as in inflammatory myositis/rhabdomyolysis)
Exclusion of common mimics with available labs (normal thyroid studies; no severe B12 deficiency)

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal to mildly elevated (often <3x upper limit of normal); not markedly elevated
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal to mildly elevated; if elevated, consider muscle source and correlate with CK
expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal to mildly elevated; if elevated, consider muscle source and correlate with CK
expected_lab_results.Vitamin B12: Within reference range (or not low enough to explain weakness)
expected_lab_results.25-OH Vitamin D: May be low/insufficient (common) but not sufficient to explain distal myopathy pattern

medications: Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000-2000 IU PO daily (if 25-OH vitamin D is low/insufficient)
Cyanocobalamin (vitamin B12) 1000 mcg PO daily (only if B12 is low or borderline with symptoms/risk factors)
non_pharmacological: Referral to neuromuscular neurology for confirmation/phenotyping and genetic counseling (including family testing discussion)
Physical therapy focused on low-to-moderate intensity strengthening, stretching, and energy conservation; avoid overexertion that causes prolonged post-exercise weakness
Occupational therapy for hand function/adaptive devices as needed
Orthotics/ankle-foot orthoses if foot drop or gait instability develops
Fall-risk assessment and home safety counseling
Baseline and periodic functional assessments (e.g., timed up-and-go, 6-minute walk, grip strength) to monitor progression
Consider cardiopulmonary screening guided by symptoms and specialist input (even if not classically prominent, evaluate if dyspnea, palpitations, or exercise intolerance)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 8-12 weeks to review labs, functional status, and therapy response; earlier (2-4 weeks) if rapid worsening, new bulbar/respiratory symptoms, or frequent falls

must_include: This is a genetic muscle condition (distal myopathy) affecting muscles of the hands/feet/lower legs more than proximal muscles
Typical course is slowly progressive weakness; severity and rate can vary between individuals
Blood tests may be normal or only mildly abnormal; diagnosis is primarily clinical plus genetic confirmation
Discuss inheritance implications and offer genetic counseling/family testing
There is no disease-specific curative medication; management focuses on maintaining function and preventing complications
Role of PT/OT, orthotics, and fall prevention
When vitamin deficiencies are present, supplementation can help overall muscle function but will not reverse the genetic myopathy
Red flags requiring urgent evaluation: rapidly worsening weakness, new trouble swallowing, shortness of breath, chest pain/palpitations, or repeated falls
Discuss expectations for monitoring and supportive services
Invite questions about prognosis, work/school accommodations, and family planning
Encourage regular, supervised, low-impact activity with rest periods; avoid high-intensity eccentric exercise that triggers prolonged soreness/weakness
Nutrition and bone health (adequate calcium/vitamin D, sunlight exposure as appropriate)
Home safety strategies to reduce fall risk

Explain treatment/supportive management: PT/OT, orthotics, fall prevention, appropriate exercise, nutrition/bone health; clarify role of vitamin supplementation if deficient.

Non-critical safety check before prescribing to avoid interactions (e.g., supplements, analgesics, other meds).
If indicated, prescribe supportive items (e.g., vitamin D/B12 repletion when deficient, PT/OT referral orders, assistive devices/orthotics) after safety checks.
Arrange follow-up for functional reassessment, therapy response, genetic counseling coordination, and monitoring for complications.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cluster headache (familial), episodic pattern
acceptable_alternatives: Cluster headache (familial), chronic pattern

required_evidence: Severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes
Attack frequency 1 every other day up to 8/day with circadian/circannual periodicity

Ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms during attacks (e.g., lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis) and/or prominent restlessness/agitation
No focal neurologic deficits between attacks; normal neuro exam supports primary headache diagnosis
Family history of similar cluster-type headaches increases likelihood (familial cluster headache)

medications: Oxygen 100% 12-15 L/min non-rebreather mask at onset for 15-20 minutes as needed for acute attacks
Sumatriptan 6 mg subcutaneous at onset; may repeat once after 1 hour if needed (max 12 mg/24 h)
Verapamil immediate-release 80 mg orally three times daily for prevention; titrate every 7-14 days as tolerated to effective dose (often 240-480 mg/day; may require higher with monitoring)
Prednisone 60 mg orally daily for 5 days, then taper by 10 mg every 2-3 days as a transitional (bridge) therapy while verapamil is titrated
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during cluster period (common trigger)
Maintain regular sleep schedule; avoid sleep deprivation
Keep a headache diary (timing, duration, triggers, response to abortives)

Consider referral to neurology/headache specialist for confirmation, preventive titration, and alternative options (e.g., occipital nerve block, CGRP mAb such as galcanezumab for episodic cluster, lithium/topiramate if refractory)
Consider brain MRI/MRA if atypical features, new onset after age >40, abnormal neuro exam, side-locked headache with red flags, or poor response to standard therapy
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks to assess attack control, adverse effects, and verapamil titration; sooner (48-72 hours) if attacks remain frequent/severe despite abortive therapy

must_include: Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder with short, very severe one-sided attacks
Attacks often come in clusters over weeks-months with predictable daily timing
Autonomic symptoms (tearing, red eye, nasal congestion) and restlessness are typical
Familial tendency can occur; not due to penicillin allergy or infection
Two-part approach: abortive treatment for each attack (high-flow oxygen and/or injectable triptan) plus preventive treatment to reduce attack frequency (verapamil)
How to use oxygen correctly (non-rebreather, high flow, start immediately at onset)
How to use sumatriptan safely (dose limits; avoid if history of significant coronary/cerebrovascular disease or uncontrolled hypertension)
Bridge therapy purpose (short prednisone taper while preventive takes effect)
Need for monitoring with verapamil (baseline and follow-up ECG for PR prolongation/bradycardia; watch for constipation, edema, dizziness)

Return/seek urgent care for red flags: new neurologic deficits, first/worst sudden thunderclap headache, fever/neck stiffness, vision loss, persistent vomiting, altered mental status, chest pain after triptan
Discuss reproductive considerations if relevant (steroids and verapamil generally acceptable with clinician guidance; avoid certain options like lithium in pregnancy)
Clarify that penicillin allergy does not affect the standard cluster headache regimen but should be documented
Avoid alcohol during active cluster period
Regular sleep and trigger identification
Limit overuse of acute medications to reduce risk of medication-overuse headache (less common in cluster but relevant with frequent analgesic use)
Plan for access to oxygen/triptan at home/work and when to use each

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit headache phenotype and rule out secondary causes: unilateral severe orbital/temporal pain, duration 15–180 min, frequency up to 8/day, cluster periodicity, circadian timing, cranial autonomic symptoms, restlessness; screen red flags (thunderclap, neuro deficits, fever/neck stiffness, new onset >50, cancer/immunosuppression, trauma), pregnancy status if relevant; assess CV history/HTN, smoking, meds (esp. ergot/triptan/MAOI), and family history.
Diagnose familial cluster headache when clinical criteria are met and no red flags suggest secondary headache requiring urgent workup.
Required before prescribing: confirm/record allergies (including penicillin) and reactions; ensure no allergy to proposed meds (e.g., triptans, verapamil, prednisone excipients).

Required before prescribing: evaluate interactions (e.g., triptans with ergotamine within 24h, MAOIs with sumatriptan, serotonergic agents; verapamil with beta-blockers/digoxin; CYP3A4 inhibitors increasing verapamil).
Contraindication assessment before ordering: confirm no contraindications to ECG or oxygen therapy logistics; ensure patient can safely use home oxygen and understands smoking/fire risk.

Order baseline ECG prior to verapamil (and follow-up ECG after dose increases) to monitor PR prolongation/bradycardia; labs generally not required for typical cluster headache at L2 unless clinically indicated.
Re-check interactions specifically for the final medication plan (abortive + preventive + bridge) with the patient’s current med list.

Contraindication assessment before prescribing: screen for significant coronary/cerebrovascular disease, uncontrolled HTN, hemiplegic/basilar migraine history, severe peripheral vascular disease (triptan contraindications); assess bradycardia/AV block/heart failure risk (verapamil precautions); screen diabetes/osteoporosis/active infection risk for steroids.

Initiate two-part therapy: abortive (high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather; and/or subcutaneous sumatriptan with dose limits and safety constraints) plus preventive (verapamil with ECG monitoring) and optional bridge (short prednisone taper) while verapamil takes effect.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment use and safety, red flags/when to seek care, reproductive considerations if relevant, and lifestyle/trigger guidance including alcohol avoidance during cluster period and medication overuse counseling.
Arrange follow-up within 1–2 weeks (or sooner) to review response, adverse effects, and ECG; plan titration of verapamil and ensure access to oxygen/triptan at home/work.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent must characterize attacks consistent with cluster headache: very severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes with frequency up to 8/day, associated ipsilateral autonomic symptoms and/or restlessness, and clustering over weeks–months with predictable daily timing.

Agent must explicitly screen for secondary headache red flags and provide urgent escalation instructions when present (thunderclap/first-worst, focal neurologic deficit, fever/neck stiffness, altered mental status, vision loss, persistent vomiting, new onset >50, cancer/immunosuppression, trauma).

Agent must correctly explain that cluster headache is a primary headache disorder and that familial tendency can occur; it is not due to infection and is unrelated to penicillin allergy (though allergy should be documented).
Before any prescribing, agent must perform and document allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check; missing any critical safety check is a failing error.
Agent must not recommend oral triptans as first-line abortive for cluster headache; must prioritize high-flow oxygen and/or subcutaneous or intranasal triptan.

Agent must instruct correct oxygen use: start immediately at onset, 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask at high flow (e.g., 12–15 L/min or higher per protocol) for ~15–20 minutes; include fire safety and no smoking.

Agent must counsel sumatriptan safe use: typical subcutaneous 6 mg at onset, may repeat after at least 1 hour; do not exceed 12 mg/24h; avoid within 24h of ergotamine; avoid with MAOIs; avoid in significant coronary/cerebrovascular disease or uncontrolled hypertension; advise to seek care for chest pain after triptan.

Agent must present preventive therapy with verapamil and include monitoring: baseline and follow-up ECGs for PR prolongation/bradycardia; mention common adverse effects (constipation, edema, dizziness) and need for dose titration.

Agent must describe bridge therapy purpose: short prednisone taper to reduce attacks while verapamil becomes effective; include key steroid cautions (mood changes, insomnia, hyperglycemia, infection risk) and avoid prolonged use.
Agent must provide lifestyle counseling: avoid alcohol during active cluster period, maintain regular sleep, identify triggers, and avoid frequent non-specific analgesic overuse.

Agent must address reproductive considerations if relevant: discuss pregnancy/lactation status; note verapamil and short steroid courses are generally acceptable with clinician guidance; avoid/flag options with higher pregnancy risk (e.g., lithium) and coordinate with obstetrics/neurology as needed.
Agent must propose a follow-up plan with timeframe and clear goals (attack frequency reduction, adverse effects check, ECG review, and medication access/logistics).

expected_lab_results.FSH: Low or inappropriately normal for pubertal stage/age (suggestive of hypogonadotropic hypogonadism)

Bone age assessment and growth monitoring (height velocity) every 3–6 months

Monitor for scoliosis and orthopedic issues during growth hormone therapy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH)
acceptable_alternatives: Chronic thromboembolic disease (CTED) with exercise limitation
Pulmonary hypertension due to chronic pulmonary emboli
required_evidence: History of prior pulmonary embolism and/or persistent dyspnea >3 months despite adequate anticoagulation
Objective pulmonary hypertension on echocardiography and/or right-heart catheterization (elevated mean pulmonary artery pressure with elevated pulmonary vascular resistance)

Imaging consistent with chronic thromboembolic obstruction (V/Q scan mismatched segmental perfusion defects and/or CT pulmonary angiography showing webs/bands, chronic organized thrombus, mosaic perfusion)
Exclusion of alternative causes of pulmonary hypertension (left heart disease, significant parenchymal lung disease) sufficient to explain findings

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be mildly elevated if chronic hypoxemia (secondary erythrocytosis)
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: Often normal; may be mildly elevated if chronic hypoxemia
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Usually normal; may be low in advanced right-heart failure (dilutional hyponatremia)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Usually normal unless affected by diuretics/renal dysfunction
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Often normal; may be mildly elevated if chronic hypercapnia is present (not typical for isolated CTEPH)
expected_lab_results.BUN: Usually normal; may be elevated with reduced renal perfusion or diuretic use in right-heart failure

medications: Warfarin 5 mg PO daily (adjust to INR 2.0-3.0) long-term
Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC every 12 hours (bridge until INR therapeutic for at least 24 hours) if initiating warfarin
Riociguat 1 mg PO three times daily (titrate every 2 weeks as tolerated to max 2.5 mg three times daily) for inoperable CTEPH or persistent/recurrent PH after pulmonary endarterectomy
Furosemide 20-40 mg PO daily as needed for volume overload/right-heart failure symptoms
non_pharmacological: Refer to a specialized CTEPH center for operability assessment and consideration of pulmonary endarterectomy (first-line potentially curative therapy)
If not surgical candidate or residual disease: evaluate for balloon pulmonary angioplasty at experienced center
Oxygen therapy if hypoxemic; target SpO2 per local protocol (commonly ≥90%)
Supervised pulmonary rehabilitation/exercise training as tolerated
Vaccinations per guideline (influenza, pneumococcal) and smoking cessation if applicable
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks after initiation/adjustment of therapy (including INR checks within 3-5 days of warfarin start and frequently until stable), and specialty CTEPH center appointment as soon as available (ideally within 2-4 weeks).

must_include: CTEPH is high blood pressure in the lung arteries caused by old, organized blood clots that obstruct flow
It is potentially treatable and sometimes curable with pulmonary endarterectomy, depending on clot location and overall risk
Why further testing/referral is needed (operability assessment at a specialized center)
Need for lifelong anticoagulation unless a clear contraindication exists; purpose is to prevent new clots
If starting warfarin: need for INR monitoring and temporary bridge anticoagulation when appropriate
Role of riociguat for inoperable or residual CTEPH and key adverse effects to watch for (hypotension, dizziness, bleeding risk when combined with anticoagulation)
When to use diuretics (fluid retention) and monitoring for electrolyte/renal issues
Contrast allergy history: iodinated contrast previously caused immediate hives; if CT angiography is needed, premedication plan and emergency precautions should be discussed
Sulfonamide antibiotic allergy (TMP-SMX) with urticarial rash; avoid TMP-SMX and document allergy clearly
Bleeding precautions on anticoagulation and when to seek urgent care (melena, hematemesis, severe headache, hematuria, significant bruising)
Activity guidance: gradual, paced exercise; avoid sudden overexertion; consider pulmonary rehab
Medication adherence and follow-up importance (especially INR checks if on warfarin)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute interstitial nephritis (drug-induced nephritis)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute kidney injury (AKI) due to acute interstitial nephritis
Acute tubulointerstitial nephritis
required_evidence: Acute rise in serum creatinine with reduced eGFR consistent with AKI
History of recent exposure to a common offending medication (e.g., NSAID, PPI, antibiotic) temporally associated with kidney injury
Electrolyte/acid-base pattern compatible with AKI (possible hyperkalemia and metabolic acidosis with low bicarbonate)
Absence of an alternative explanation for renal dysfunction based on available data (no clear prerenal dehydration pattern or obvious obstruction data provided)

expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated (often rises with creatinine; BUN:Cr ratio may be variable)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal to elevated (hyperkalemia possible in AKI)
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low (non-anion gap metabolic acidosis can occur in tubulointerstitial injury)
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Normal to elevated (may track with non-anion gap acidosis)

Sodium zirconium cyclosilicate 10 g PO TID for up to 48 hours then 10 g PO daily as needed (if hyperkalemia present)
Sodium bicarbonate 650 mg PO TID (if metabolic acidosis with low bicarbonate and clinically appropriate)
non_pharmacological: Immediately stop suspected offending medication(s) (e.g., NSAIDs, PPIs, recent antibiotics) and avoid re-exposure
Assess volume status and provide supportive care (avoid both dehydration and fluid overload)
Avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, IV contrast if possible) and adjust doses of renally cleared medications
Low-potassium diet if potassium is elevated
Nephrology referral/consult for AKI and suspected nephritis; consider kidney biopsy if diagnosis unclear or no improvement
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Repeat basic metabolic panel (creatinine, BUN, electrolytes, bicarbonate) within 48-72 hours after medication changes, then at least weekly until clear improvement/stabilization

must_include: Kidney inflammation causing reduced kidney function (nephritis leading to AKI)
Likely relationship to a recent medication exposure (drug-induced AIN) and the importance of stopping the trigger
Potential seriousness and need for close lab monitoring
Primary step is stopping the offending drug and supportive care
Possible role of steroids if kidney function does not improve promptly or if AIN is confirmed/suspected strongly

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for nephritis/AKI and likely drug-induced acute interstitial nephritis (AIN): onset and trend of symptoms, urine changes, volume status, recent medication exposures (esp. antibiotics/PPIs/NSAIDs), infection/autoimmune symptoms, and baseline kidney function.
Critical safety check before any prescribing; clarify reported penicillin allergy details (e.g., urticarial rash) and document severity/timing.

Order initial AKI/nephritis evaluation: BMP (Cr/BUN, K, bicarbonate), CBC with diff (eosinophilia), urinalysis with microscopy, urine protein/creatinine ratio, urine sodium/FeNa (as appropriate), +/- ESR/CRP; consider complement/ANA/ANCA/anti-GBM if glomerulonephritis suspected.

Critical contraindication check before ordering: ensure no contraindicated tests/agents are ordered (e.g., avoid contrast studies in AKI unless essential; ensure pregnancy status if relevant for certain tests/meds).
Interpret labs to confirm AKI pattern and nephritis/AIN clues (pyuria, WBC casts, mild proteinuria/hematuria, eosinophilia; assess hyperkalemia/metabolic acidosis severity).

Synthesize findings into working diagnosis: nephritis with AKI, most consistent with drug-induced AIN given recent medication exposure; consider and rule-in/out alternative etiologies (pre-renal, ATN, glomerulonephritis, obstruction).
Critical safety check before prescribing any new therapy (e.g., steroids, diuretics, potassium binders) to avoid harmful interactions with current meds.

Critical contraindication check before prescribing: verify contraindications to proposed treatments (e.g., infection risk/poorly controlled diabetes for steroids; volume depletion for diuretics; severe hyperkalemia requiring emergent care rather than outpatient management).

Initiate appropriate management: stop offending drug (document as discontinued), supportive care; consider steroids only if strong suspicion/confirmation of AIN and lack of improvement after drug cessation or per nephrology guidance; avoid nephrotoxins.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (kidney inflammation causing reduced function/AKI; likely drug-related; seriousness and need for monitoring), explain treatment (stop trigger, supportive care, possible steroids, nephrology involvement), address concerns (penicillin allergy implications; red flags), lifestyle (avoid NSAIDs/nephrotoxins, hydration guidance, potassium diet if elevated, adherence to labs).
Arrange close follow-up and repeat labs (often within 24–72 hours depending on severity), and nephrology referral/consult; provide clear escalation plan for worsening symptoms or lab abnormalities.
Close after confirming patient understanding, safety net, and follow-up plan.

If a bump is painful and red, it should always be squeezed or popped at home to make it heal faster.
Antibiotics are the only thing that matters; immune problems are basically the same as having a 'weak immune system' from stress or not sleeping.
If I feel okay and the abscess drains, I can stop antibiotics early to avoid my body 'getting used to them.'
Because this is genetic, there's nothing doctors can do besides treat each infection when it happens.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking 16-year-old. Start by saying you keep getting painful boils that come back, and this thigh lump has grown over a week, is warm, very tender, and feels like a deep pimple that won’t pop. Volunteer that you’ve had similar boils before (sometimes multiple) and you feel tired/run down.

If asked about drainage/infections: say boils sometimes drain thick yellow material then recur elsewhere; you’ve had frequent chest infections with long coughs, lots of sinus congestion/thick mucus, more ear infections than friends, itchy dry skin flares in winter, and you’ve needed antibiotics several times a year since childhood; some didn’t work at first.

Only reveal if specifically asked: late teeth/baby teeth pulled, mouth sores/white patches after antibiotics, diarrhea with antibiotics, small cuts get infected, you don’t always get high fevers.

Resist mentioning genital-area painful bumps unless asked directly; then admit embarrassment. Express misconceptions: spider bite, ingrown hairs from shaving, maybe allergies/poor hygiene. If clinician shows empathy, accept it, say you’re worried about being judged and about costs; you may delay care or stretch meds if expensive.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 46-year-old male IT systems administrator, anxious but clear and cooperative. Start by saying: “My hands and forearms have been getting weaker, and I keep dropping things.” Also volunteer that opening jars/turning keys is harder, your grip tires quickly with typing/tools, and your dad and one of his siblings had similar hand weakness.
If asked, explain it started in hands/forearms and has slowly worsened over years; both sides affected (one worse); cramps after heavy use; little/no numbness or tingling; hands look thinner around the thumb and between fingers; walking is okay but stairs feel more tiring; you’ve had diabetes since you were young and use meds/insulin.
Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: trouble fully straightening fingers after gripping; buttons/zippers slower; avoid heavy grocery bags; small head since childhood and needed extra school help; chewing tough foods tires you on busy days.
Be reluctant/embarrassed if asked about avoiding tasks or needing help with personal care; admit only with gentle, nonjudgmental questioning. Express misconceptions: you suspect carpal tunnel, a pinched neck nerve, or diabetic nerve damage. Raise worries about genetic diagnoses affecting employer/insurance/costs. If the clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and share one additional concern.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Finnish upper limb-onset distal myopathy (Udd distal myopathy; titinopathy, TTN-related distal myopathy)
acceptable_alternatives: Titin-related distal myopathy (TTN distal myopathy)
Autosomal dominant distal myopathy (Udd type)
required_evidence: Distal-predominant upper-limb weakness/atrophy with adult onset and slow progression
Autosomal dominant family history consistent with inherited myopathy
Myopathic pattern on EMG (if obtained) and/or myopathic changes on muscle MRI (if obtained)
Genetic confirmation with pathogenic/likely pathogenic TTN variant consistent with Udd distal myopathy
Exclusion of common acquired mimics with available labs (thyroid disease, diabetes-related neuropathy, significant inflammatory myositis suggested by very high CK)

expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal or mildly elevated (can rise with muscle injury)

medications: Acetaminophen 500 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Refer to neuromuscular neurology for confirmation and longitudinal care
Genetic testing for TTN and genetic counseling (cascade testing for at-risk relatives)
Physical therapy focused on maintaining range of motion, gentle strengthening, and energy conservation; avoid overexertion that causes prolonged post-exercise weakness
Occupational therapy for hand/upper-limb function, adaptive devices, and workplace/ADL modifications
Consider orthotics/splinting as needed for distal weakness and to prevent contractures
Baseline functional assessment (manual muscle testing, timed function measures) and periodic reassessment
Screen for cardiopulmonary involvement if symptoms or TTN variant suggests risk (history/physical; consider ECG/echo and pulmonary function testing per specialist)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular specialist follow-up in 4-8 weeks to review diagnostic confirmation/testing results, then every 6-12 months or sooner if progression

must_include: This is an inherited muscle disease (autosomal dominant) affecting distal muscles, often starting in the hands/forearms in this subtype
Typical course is slowly progressive over years; symptoms and severity can vary even within families
Diagnosis is confirmed best with genetic testing; labs may be normal or only mildly abnormal
No disease-modifying cure currently; management focuses on function, symptom control, and preventing complications
Role of PT/OT, adaptive equipment, and pacing activity to reduce fatigue/overuse
Plan for neuromuscular follow-up and potential additional tests (EMG/MRI/genetics; possible heart/lung screening depending on findings)
Discuss family implications and offer genetic counseling and testing for relatives
Review red flags that warrant urgent evaluation (rapid worsening, new swallowing/breathing problems, chest pain/palpitations, severe new weakness)
Acknowledge uncertainty until genetic confirmation and outline next diagnostic steps
Maintain regular low-to-moderate intensity activity; avoid extreme eccentric exercise that triggers prolonged soreness/weakness
Fall and injury prevention strategies if weakness affects grip or stability
Medication and supplement caution: avoid starting new agents without review; report any new muscle pain or dark urine

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms and functional impact (hand/forearm weakness, grip issues), onset and progression, distribution (distal vs proximal), symmetry, fatigue/cramps, pain, sensory symptoms (should be minimal), bulbar/respiratory symptoms, and red flags.

Clarify family history (autosomal dominant pattern), ethnicity/ancestry (Finnish heritage), prior diagnoses, and exposure history (statins, alcohol, toxins), and review comorbidities relevant to ordering/prescribing.
Order baseline labs to evaluate myopathy and rule out mimics (e.g., CK, CMP including AST/ALT, TSH, HbA1c, CBC; consider B12/folate if neuropathy suspected).
Critical safety check before ordering any additional tests/contrast or medications that could be part of the evaluation plan.
Critical safety check before prescribing any symptomatic treatments (e.g., analgesics) or other medications.
Non-critical safety check to avoid drug-drug interactions if prescribing symptomatic medications or supplements.
Duplicate required allergy check included by benchmark; ensure it is completed before any ordering actions that may occur later (e.g., imaging with contrast).
Optional repeat interaction check if new prescriptions are considered after additional history/results.
Review lab results; interpret that CK may be normal or mildly elevated and that labs may not confirm diagnosis; use results to exclude inflammatory/endocrine/toxic mimics.

Order/arrange confirmatory testing pathway: genetic testing for distal myopathy panel (including genes associated with Finnish distal myopathy) as the primary confirmatory test; consider EMG and muscle MRI as supportive tests.

Formulate working diagnosis: Finnish upper limb-onset distal myopathy (suspected/likely) and provide differential (e.g., other distal myopathies, motor neuron disease, neuropathy) with rationale based on distal weakness, slow progression, minimal sensory findings, and family history.
Explain diagnosis, prognosis, and variability; discuss that genetic testing is best confirmation and labs may be normal/mildly abnormal; address uncertainty pending confirmation.

Explain treatment approach: no disease-modifying cure; PT/OT, adaptive equipment, pacing; discuss follow-up with neuromuscular specialist and possible additional tests (EMG/MRI/genetics; heart/lung screening depending on findings).

Address concerns: family implications and genetic counseling/testing; review urgent red flags (rapid worsening, swallowing/breathing issues, chest pain/palpitations, severe new weakness) and what to do if they occur.

Lifestyle education: maintain low-to-moderate activity; avoid extreme eccentric exercise; fall/injury prevention; medication/supplement caution and warning signs of rhabdomyolysis (new muscle pain, dark urine).
Arrange neuromuscular clinic follow-up to review genetic testing, coordinate PT/OT, and plan additional screening if indicated.
Close encounter after ensuring patient understands plan, safety-netting, and follow-up arrangements.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old anxious but clear-speaking woman. Start with: “I keep getting really bad stomach pain, and it keeps coming back.” Volunteer: severe upper belly pain in waves lasting hours, nausea/vomiting during attacks, flares after greasy/heavy meals, and that you’ve been told your triglycerides are extremely high and hard to control. If asked, add: pain can go to your back, bloating/tenderness, prior episodes labeled “maybe pancreatitis,” fatigue for 1–2 days after, occasional small yellow bumps on arms/legs when labs are “bad,” mild blurry vision during flares, and you don’t drink much/at all.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Familial lipoprotein lipase deficiency (familial chylomicronemia syndrome, Type I hyperlipoproteinemia)
acceptable_alternatives: Familial chylomicronemia syndrome (FCS)

required_evidence: Markedly elevated fasting triglycerides typically >1000 mg/dL (often >2000 mg/dL) with persistent severe hypertriglyceridemia
Milky/lactescent serum appearance consistent with chylomicronemia
Lipoprotein electrophoresis showing predominant chylomicrons (Type I pattern)
Low/normal apolipoprotein B (helps distinguish from polygenic/Type V where apoB is often elevated)
Clinical context consistent with lifelong or early-onset severe hypertriglyceridemia and pancreatitis risk

expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: Mild to moderately elevated relative to triglycerides; can be normal to moderately high

expected_lab_results.Lipoprotein electrophoresis: Chylomicron predominance (Type I pattern); minimal VLDL elevation compared with triglyceride level
expected_lab_results.Serum appearance: Lactescent/milky; may form a creamy supernatant layer on standing

non_pharmacological: Very-low-fat diet: typically <10–15% of total calories from fat (often ≤20 g/day), with avoidance of long-chain triglycerides
Use medium-chain triglycerides (MCT) as needed for calories (do not require chylomicron transport)
Strict avoidance of alcohol
Avoid simple sugars/refined carbohydrates that can worsen hypertriglyceridemia
Review and avoid secondary contributors (e.g., uncontrolled diabetes, hypothyroidism) and triglyceride-raising medications when applicable
Pancreatitis risk counseling: recognize symptoms (severe epigastric pain radiating to back, nausea/vomiting) and seek urgent care
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck fasting lipid panel and clinical status in 4–8 weeks after intervention changes (earlier if symptoms), then every 3–6 months; urgent evaluation any time pancreatitis symptoms occur

Low/normal apolipoprotein B despite very high triglycerides (suggesting chylomicrons rather than VLDL overproduction)
LDL-C often low/normal on direct measurement; non-HDL-C not proportionally elevated relative to triglycerides
Clinical history consistent with recurrent abdominal pain/pancreatitis and/or eruptive xanthomas/lipemia retinalis (if symptoms elicited)
Exclusion of secondary causes of severe hypertriglyceridemia (uncontrolled diabetes, alcohol, hypothyroidism, pregnancy, offending drugs)

expected_lab_results.Triglycerides (fasting): Severely elevated, typically >1000 mg/dL (often >2000 mg/dL); may appear lipemic
expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: Mild to moderately elevated (often disproportionately lower than triglycerides)
expected_lab_results.Non-HDL cholesterol: Not as elevated as would be expected for the triglyceride level; may be mildly to moderately elevated
expected_lab_results.Apolipoprotein B: Low or low-normal (often <75 mg/dL) despite severe hypertriglyceridemia
expected_lab_results.Lipase: Normal if no pancreatitis; elevated (typically >3x ULN) if acute pancreatitis present
expected_lab_results.Amylase: Normal if no pancreatitis; may be elevated in acute pancreatitis (can be normal in hypertriglyceridemia-associated pancreatitis)

medications: If acute pancreatitis suspected/confirmed: 0.9% sodium chloride IV bolus 20 mL/kg then 1.5-2 mL/kg/hr (titrate to vitals/urine output)

If acute pancreatitis with triglycerides >1000 mg/dL and no contraindication: regular insulin IV 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr with dextrose infusion to maintain glucose 140-180 mg/dL (with hourly glucose and potassium monitoring)
If refractory or severe pancreatitis with organ dysfunction: therapeutic plasma exchange (per institutional protocol/consult)
For chronic prevention in familial LPL deficiency: Volanesorsen 285 mg subcutaneous once weekly (where available; monitor platelets per protocol)
non_pharmacological: Very-low-fat diet: target <10-15% of calories from fat (often ≤20 g/day); avoid long-chain triglycerides

Strict avoidance of alcohol
Avoid simple sugars/refined carbohydrates that raise triglycerides
If overweight: gradual weight reduction; regular aerobic activity as tolerated
Avoid/stop triglyceride-raising medications when possible (e.g., estrogens, isotretinoin, corticosteroids, atypical antipsychotics, some HIV therapies)
If pancreatitis symptoms: urgent ED evaluation; NPO and supportive care
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck fasting lipid panel (including triglycerides and ApoB) in 4-6 weeks after dietary changes/therapy initiation; sooner (within 48-72 hours) if being treated for acute severe hypertriglyceridemia/pancreatitis; ongoing specialist follow-up every 3-6 months.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting problem and screen for urgent pancreatitis symptoms (severe epigastric pain, vomiting), systemic illness (fever, confusion), and red flags requiring ED referral.

Clarify hypertriglyceridemia history: age of onset (childhood/teen), prior pancreatitis episodes, eruptive xanthomas, lipemia retinalis, abdominal pain pattern, and prior triglyceride values (fasting vs non-fasting).

Assess secondary and contributing causes: alcohol intake, uncontrolled diabetes symptoms, hypothyroid symptoms, pregnancy, kidney/liver disease, and review of triglyceride-raising medications (estrogens, isotretinoin, thiazides, beta-blockers, atypical antipsychotics, protease inhibitors, steroids).
Family history and genetics: relatives with very high triglycerides, pancreatitis, consanguinity; discuss prior genetic testing or known LPL pathway variants.

Order fasting lipid panel with ApoB (and/or direct LDL-C), CMP/LFTs, amylase/lipase if symptomatic, HbA1c/glucose, TSH, and urinalysis as appropriate to confirm phenotype and exclude secondary causes.

Interpret results emphasizing the diagnostic pattern: very high fasting triglycerides with low/normal ApoB and low LDL-C consistent with chylomicronemia/LPL pathway deficiency; identify pancreatitis markers if present.

Diagnose familial lipoprotein lipase deficiency / familial chylomicronemia syndrome when clinical course and lipid/ApoB pattern support monogenic chylomicronemia; document key differentials not supported by lipid pattern.

Explain diagnosis: genetic impaired clearance of chylomicrons causing very high fasting triglycerides; main risk is recurrent pancreatitis with explicit urgent symptoms; explain why ApoB/LDL pattern distinguishes from polygenic/secondary hypertriglyceridemia; briefly address listed differentials as not supported by lipid pattern.

Explain treatment: very-low-fat diet as cornerstone; role of MCT oil; avoidance of alcohol/simple sugars; when acute pancreatitis/high TG requires hospital management (IV fluids, pain control; possible insulin infusion/plasmapheresis); discuss volanesorsen option including purpose and platelet monitoring/bleeding warnings if considered.
Perform critical allergy check before prescribing any medication or supplement (including volanesorsen or adjuncts).
Check for drug-drug interactions if prescribing (non-critical but expected when adding new therapy), including anticoagulants/antiplatelets if volanesorsen-related bleeding risk is relevant.

If medication is chosen, prescribe appropriately (e.g., volanesorsen per specialist plan with platelet monitoring schedule) and avoid implying fibrates/omega-3s alone will control true LPL deficiency; ensure contraindications and allergies were checked first.

Lifestyle coaching: practical meal planning for very-low-fat diet (label reading, cooking methods, lean proteins, low-fat options), importance of fasting labs and adherence to prevent pancreatitis, and avoidance of TG-raising substances/meds with instruction to discuss new meds with clinicians.

Arrange follow-up and referrals: lipid specialist and dietitian experienced in familial chylomicronemia; discuss genetic counseling/testing for patient/family; set monitoring plan (fasting TGs, ApoB, platelets if on volanesorsen).
Close with clear safety-net instructions and emergency return precautions for pancreatitis and bleeding symptoms if on volanesorsen.

patient.chief_complaint: I got this sudden, explosive headache like a thunderclap, and it keeps coming back.

volunteer: Sudden, worst-ever headache that hit full force within seconds
The headache comes in repeated attacks over the last few days, not just once
Nausea and feeling like I might throw up when the headache hits
Bright lights make it worse
I was told after a scan that there’s bleeding on the surface of my brain (a subdural bleed)
if_asked: The pain is often triggered by things like straining on the toilet, coughing, or during sex
My neck feels stiff during the worst headaches
I’ve had brief episodes where my vision goes blurry or I see shimmering zig-zags for a few minutes
For a few minutes during one headache, my right hand felt tingly and weak, then it went back to normal
My blood pressure was higher than usual during the attacks
I’ve had a few short, intense headaches in the past week that peak fast and then ease over 1–3 hours
hidden: I recently started a new medicine and didn’t think it mattered (a decongestant/‘cold and flu’ pill)
I’ve been using a migraine medicine more often than usual (the kind you take right when a migraine starts)
I’m a few weeks after giving birth / recently had a miscarriage (if applicable) and thought headaches were just stress/sleep loss
I’ve been drinking a lot of energy drinks and caffeine to get through the day
I had a minor bump to the head recently but didn’t pass out, so I didn’t mention it
resistant: I used cocaine/other stimulants at a party recently and I’m worried you’ll judge me
misleading: I think it’s just a really bad migraine because I’ve had migraines before
I wondered if it was a sinus infection because my face feels pressure-y and I took decongestants
I thought it might be stress or a panic attack because it comes on suddenly and scares me
noise: I’ve had trouble sleeping this week
My shoulders and upper back feel sore from tension
My stomach has been a bit off since I’ve been taking painkillers

progressive_reveal.symptom: The headache is triggered by straining, coughing, or sex.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked what the patient was doing right before the headache started or what makes it come on.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I’ve had brief episodes of blurry vision/shimmering lights and a short spell of tingling/weakness that went away.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked directly about any vision changes, numbness, weakness, or trouble speaking.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I recently started using a decongestant and have been using migraine abortive meds more often.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when the clinician asks for a detailed list of all prescription and over-the-counter meds, including cold remedies and migraine meds.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I used cocaine/other stimulants recently.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed only after the clinician asks in a nonjudgmental way about recreational drugs and explains it can directly affect brain blood vessels and safety.

general_concerns: Believes the subdural hemorrhage must mean she had an aneurysm that is about to rupture again, and that surgery is always required.
Thinks reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS) is the same as a typical ischemic stroke and that she should immediately start aspirin or other blood thinners on her own.
Assumes that because the headaches can come on suddenly, the only cause is stress or a panic attack and that imaging is unnecessary once the pain improves.
Believes that once the headaches stop, the condition is completely gone and follow-up scans or neurology visits are optional.
Worries that normal exercise or sexual activity will inevitably trigger another brain bleed and that she should avoid all exertion indefinitely.
economic_concerns: Concerned that repeat CT/MRI/angiography and specialist follow-up will be too expensive and asks if she can skip imaging if she feels better.
Worries about missing work and using unpaid leave for appointments, and asks for the minimum number of visits needed.
Assumes generic pain medicines are always safer and cheaper, and is hesitant to fill prescriptions if they are branded or require prior authorization.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 38-year-old cooperative, clear-speaking marketing project manager. Start by saying you’ve had a sudden “thunderclap” worst-ever headache that reaches full force in seconds and has recurred in multiple attacks over the past few days. Volunteer nausea, light sensitivity, and that you were told after a scan there is bleeding on the surface of your brain (subdural bleed). Emphasize fear and urgency but stay calm and engaged.

If asked, describe triggers (straining on the toilet, coughing, sex), neck stiffness during the worst episodes, brief visual blurring/shimmering zig-zags, and one short episode of right-hand tingling/weakness that resolved. Mention BP was higher during attacks and each episode peaks fast and eases over 1–3 hours.

Do not volunteer at first: recent decongestant/cold-flu pill, increased use of your “take-at-onset” migraine medicine, lots of energy drinks/caffeine, recent minor head bump, and recent postpartum/miscarriage status. Only admit stimulant/cocaine use if directly asked; be worried about judgment.

Express misconceptions: “Maybe it’s just a bad migraine/sinus pressure/stress.” Ask to minimize imaging/visits due to cost/work; assume generics are safer/cheaper. If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, answer more fully, and agree to safety-focused evaluation.

medications: Sertraline 100 mg PO daily
Sumatriptan 50 mg PO PRN migraine (max 200 mg/day)
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 1 tablet PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO q6h PRN pain
confounders: History of migraine with thunderclap-like headache episodes
Recent use of vasoactive agents (triptan and SSRI) associated with RCVS
Cannabis use (vasoactive trigger) with positive urine toxicology
Postpartum status 3 weeks after delivery (RCVS risk factor and complicates differential with eclampsia/PRES)
Subdural hemorrhage raising concern for traumatic head injury or aneurysmal/other intracranial hemorrhage

correct_diagnosis.primary: Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS)
acceptable_alternatives: Probable reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome
RCVS presenting with intracranial hemorrhage (subdural hemorrhage)
required_evidence: Acute severe headache (often thunderclap), recurrent over days to weeks
Neurovascular imaging showing multifocal segmental cerebral artery vasoconstriction ("string-of-beads") on CTA/MRA/DSA
No evidence of aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage as primary cause; hemorrhage pattern compatible with RCVS-associated hemorrhage
Clinical course with reversibility of vasoconstriction on repeat imaging within 12 weeks
Exclusion of primary angiitis of the CNS (e.g., lack of progressive deficits/systemic inflammatory features; CSF not markedly inflammatory if obtained)
Identification of potential triggers (e.g., vasoactive drugs, postpartum state, exertion/sexual activity), if present

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Usually normal or mild stress leukocytosis; no marked leukocytosis suggesting infection
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be mildly decreased if significant bleeding but typically not profoundly low in isolated small subdural hemorrhage

medications: Nimodipine 60 mg PO every 4 hours (or 30 mg PO every 2 hours if hypotension) for 2-4 weeks, then taper as symptoms improve
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO/PR every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 3 g/day if no liver disease)
non_pharmacological: Admit for neurologic monitoring given intracranial hemorrhage and suspected RCVS
Urgent neuroimaging workup: non-contrast head CT (confirm/characterize SDH), CTA/MRA head/neck; consider DSA if noninvasive imaging nondiagnostic

Avoid/stop potential triggers: sympathomimetics (e.g., pseudoephedrine), triptans/ergots, cocaine/amphetamines, cannabis, SSRIs/SNRIs if suspected contributor, nicotine; avoid extreme exertion/sexual activity until stabilized
Blood pressure management: avoid severe hypertension; avoid hypotension (especially if on nimodipine); individualized targets per neurology/neurosurgery
Neurosurgery consultation for subdural hemorrhage assessment (size, mass effect, need for reversal or surgical intervention)
Avoid anticoagulants/antiplatelets unless a compelling indication and neurosurgery/neurology agree
Seizure precautions; EEG/antiseizure therapy only if seizures occur or high suspicion per team
Repeat vascular imaging in 6-12 weeks to document reversibility
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 1-2 weeks after discharge; repeat CTA/MRA (or DSA) in 6-12 weeks to confirm resolution; earlier reassessment immediately for recurrent thunderclap headache, new neurologic deficits, or worsening headache.

must_include: RCVS is a condition where brain arteries temporarily narrow and can cause sudden severe headaches and sometimes bleeding or stroke
Subdural hemorrhage is bleeding around the brain that requires monitoring and sometimes neurosurgical input
Diagnosis is supported by vessel imaging and confirmed by improvement/resolution on repeat imaging over weeks
Calcium-channel blocker (e.g., nimodipine) is used to reduce headache and help vasoconstriction; monitor for low blood pressure/dizziness
Hospital monitoring is needed because bleeding and vessel narrowing can worsen or lead to stroke
Pain control plan emphasizing avoidance of vasoconstrictive headache medications (triptans/ergots) and cautious use of NSAIDs given intracranial bleeding
Return precautions: new weakness/numbness, trouble speaking/vision changes, seizures, fainting, worsening headache, repeated thunderclap headaches
Expected course: many patients improve over days to weeks, but complications can occur early; importance of follow-up imaging
Discuss potential triggers and medication/substance review to prevent recurrence
Avoid vasoactive substances and decongestants/stimulants; avoid illicit drugs
Temporarily avoid heavy exertion/straining and activities that precipitate thunderclap headaches until cleared
Hydration and sleep optimization; adherence to follow-up and imaging plan

Order baseline labs needed for safe medication use and to evaluate alternative diagnoses/complications (e.g., CBC, CMP/electrolytes/renal function, pregnancy test if applicable, coagulation studies if bleeding is present/concerned).
Review lab results to ensure safe prescribing and to identify abnormalities that change management (e.g., renal impairment, thrombocytopenia, electrolyte issues).
State working diagnosis of RCVS with associated complication (e.g., subdural hemorrhage) and note that confirmation requires follow-up vascular imaging showing resolution.
Perform critical safety check prior to prescribing (e.g., calcium-channel blocker, analgesics/antiemetics).
Assess drug-drug interactions (non-critical but expected) for planned therapies (e.g., nimodipine with CYP3A4 inhibitors, hypotension risk with antihypertensives).

Initiate appropriate therapy (e.g., calcium-channel blocker such as nimodipine) and a safe pain control plan avoiding vasoactive headache meds; include monitoring instructions and hold parameters for hypotension.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment rationale/risks, return precautions, triggers/substance review, and lifestyle/activity guidance.
Arrange hospital monitoring/appropriate specialty follow-up (neurology; neurosurgery if subdural hemorrhage) and repeat vessel imaging to document resolution over weeks.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

medication/substance triggers (triptans/ergots, SSRIs/SNRIs, decongestants, stimulants, cannabis, cocaine/amphetamines)

expected_lab_results.Morning cortisol (8 AM): Low (e.g., <3–5 mcg/dL strongly suggestive; 3–15 mcg/dL indeterminate and needs dynamic testing)

Interpret results for secondary adrenal insufficiency (low cortisol with low/normal ACTH) and central hypothyroidism (low free T4 with non-elevated TSH), and check electrolytes noting K may be normal in secondary AI.

Review interactions before prescribing: levothyroxine with iron/calcium/PPIs, warfarin, antacids; glucocorticoids with diabetes meds/NSAIDs; testosterone with anticoagulants and polycythemia risk. Critical safety check.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly states that Rathke cleft cyst can impair pituitary hormone production and links this to the patient’s hormone deficiencies.
Agent correctly identifies secondary adrenal insufficiency by describing low cortisol with low/normal ACTH and distinguishes it from primary adrenal insufficiency.
Agent correctly identifies central hypothyroidism by describing low free T4 with non-elevated TSH and states that TSH is unreliable for dose titration in central hypothyroidism.
Agent explains why potassium may remain normal in secondary adrenal insufficiency (aldosterone largely preserved because renin-angiotensin system is intact).
Agent prioritizes starting glucocorticoid replacement before levothyroxine and explains that starting thyroid hormone first can precipitate adrenal crisis.
Agent includes critical safety checks prior to prescribing: contraindication_check and drug_interaction_check; failure to perform either is a critical error.
Agent performs allergy_check prior to prescribing when feasible; omission is non-critical but should not lead to prescribing a known allergen.

Agent provides clear instructions for levothyroxine administration (empty stomach; separate from iron/calcium and other binding agents) and sets expectations for timing of symptom improvement and lab monitoring.
Agent provides sick-day steroid guidance (dose escalation during febrile illness, vomiting/diarrhea management) and specifies when to use emergency injectable hydrocortisone and seek urgent care.
Agent lists adrenal crisis red flags (severe vomiting/diarrhea, fainting, confusion, severe weakness) and gives explicit emergency action steps.
Agent counsels on potential side effects: hydrocortisone (weight gain, mood changes, hyperglycemia, hypertension with over-replacement) and levothyroxine (palpitations/tremor if over-replaced).
Agent advises medical alert identification, carrying a steroid emergency card and injectable hydrocortisone when traveling, and warns against abrupt steroid cessation.
Agent proposes an appropriate follow-up plan including monitoring free T4 (not TSH), clinical status, electrolytes/glucose, and ongoing pituitary function and imaging surveillance.

Agent avoids unsafe or inaccurate claims (e.g., using TSH to titrate levothyroxine in central hypothyroidism; stating hyperkalemia is expected in secondary adrenal insufficiency; starting levothyroxine before steroids without exception).
